title: Evidence tiers
content: PinWise rates each compound's evidence on a four-tier scale shown as a letter badge. Tier A — FDA-approved (human): approved by the FDA for human use. Tier B — human trials, not approved: has human clinical-trial data but is not FDA-approved. Tier C — preclinical / no completed human trials: evidence is animal/lab or very limited uncontrolled human data. Tier D — precursor/topical evidence, injected off-label: the human evidence is for a different form (topical or oral) while injectable use is off-label and unstudied. Anything below Tier A carries a strong research-only disclaimer. A higher tier means better-studied, not safe for you.

title: WADA prohibited status
content: WADA-prohibited means the compound is on the World Anti-Doping Agency's prohibited list — banned for drug-tested athletes. It says nothing about legality or safety for the general public; it is specifically about competitive/tested sport.

title: Half-life
content: A compound's half-life is how long the body takes to clear about half of a dose. It sets dosing frequency and how long the compound lingers: a short half-life (minutes to hours) usually means more frequent dosing; a long half-life (days) supports weekly dosing and means the compound accumulates and washes out slowly. Half-life is a population average and individuals vary.

title: Storage and beyond-use dating
content: A reconstituted (mixed) peptide vial should be stored REFRIGERATED at about 2-8 C (36-46 F), protected from light. Do not store a mixed vial at room temperature for extended periods. Per US Pharmacopeia (USP) beyond-use guidance for multi-dose vials, a reconstituted vial should be discarded about 28 days after mixing, due to the risk of bacterial or fungal growth over time — this is PinWise's recommended discard window. Lyophilized (freeze-dried, unmixed) powder is far more stable and is kept cold or frozen; the ~28-day clock starts only once it is reconstituted.

title: Reconstitution and units
content: Reconstitution means adding bacteriostatic water (BAC water) to a lyophilized powder to make an injectable solution. The core relationship is: concentration = total mass of peptide divided by volume of water added. For compounded GLP-1 products measured in insulin units, the identity is: units to draw = (dose in mcg x total volume in mL) divided by (vial mass in mg x 10). Adding more water makes the solution more dilute (more units per dose); adding less makes it more concentrated. Always double-check the arithmetic against the vial's stated strength before drawing.

title: Compounded GLP-1 unit-dosing safety
content: The FDA warned in 2024 about dosing errors with compounded GLP-1 products, where people drew 5-20x the intended dose by confusing units on an insulin syringe with milligrams or milliliters. When a product is dosed in units, the unit count depends entirely on the vial's concentration — the same 10 units can be very different doses across two differently-concentrated vials. Confirm the concentration and units-per-dose every time.

title: Not medical advice
content: PinWise is a personal record-keeping and education tool. It is not a medical device and does not diagnose, treat, or recommend doses. Nothing here — including compound facts, calculators, or assistant responses — is medical advice. Many of these compounds are research-only or not FDA-approved and evidence is often preliminary. Decisions about your health belong with a licensed healthcare professional.

title: Testosterone and TRT overview
content: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) is prescribed for diagnosed hypogonadism (clinically low testosterone with symptoms). In the US, testosterone is a Schedule III controlled substance and is prescription-only, and it is on the WADA prohibited list. TRT is a medical treatment requiring bloodwork (total and free testosterone, plus monitoring of estradiol, hematocrit, PSA, and lipids) and clinician supervision. Whether TRT is appropriate and at what dose are medical decisions, not something to self-prescribe.

title: Testosterone esters (cypionate, enanthate, propionate)
content: Injectable testosterone is esterified, which controls release speed. Testosterone cypionate is a long ester, half-life about 8 days, typically dosed weekly and often split into two smaller injections per week for steadier levels. Testosterone enanthate is a long ester, half-life roughly 4.5-7 days, used similarly to cypionate. Testosterone propionate is a short ester, half-life about 0.8 day (~20 h), needing frequent injections (often every other day) with more level fluctuation. The ester only changes release kinetics and injection frequency — the testosterone delivered is the same molecule. Longer esters mean fewer injections but slower to reach and leave steady state.

title: Injection routes for TRT (IM vs subcutaneous)
content: Testosterone is injected intramuscularly (IM) or subcutaneously (SubQ). IM traditionally uses the ventrogluteal, deltoid, or quad with a longer needle (about 23-25 gauge, 1-1.5 inch). SubQ testosterone into abdominal or thigh fat with a short insulin needle (about 27-30 gauge, half inch or shorter) is now well-supported in the literature as effective and more comfortable, and is increasingly common. Rotate sites to avoid scarring. Route and frequency are clinical decisions.

title: Estrogen management on TRT (aromatase and AIs)
content: Some testosterone converts to estradiol (E2) via the enzyme aromatase, so estradiol often rises on TRT. Estradiol is not simply bad in men — it matters for libido, erectile function, bone density, mood, and healthy lipids. Modern TRT guidance cautions AGAINST routinely crushing estradiol with aromatase inhibitors (AIs) such as anastrozole or exemestane; over-suppressing E2 commonly causes joint pain, low libido, low mood, and bone loss. AIs are prescription drugs generally reserved for genuinely elevated, symptomatic estradiol, used cautiously and guided by symptoms and labs — not as a default. This is a medical decision.

title: HCG on TRT
content: Human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) mimics luteinizing hormone (LH), signaling the testes directly. On TRT, where the body's own LH is suppressed, HCG is sometimes added under a clinician to help preserve testicular size, intratesticular testosterone, and fertility. It is a prescription medication and its use, timing, and dose are clinical decisions.

title: SERMs and post-cycle therapy (PCT)
content: Selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) include tamoxifen (Nolvadex) and clomiphene (Clomid). Tamoxifen blocks estrogen receptors in breast tissue (used against gynecomastia); clomiphene stimulates the pituitary to raise the body's own LH/FSH and thus endogenous testosterone. In the community, SERMs are used for post-cycle therapy (PCT) — trying to restart natural testosterone production after suppressive anabolic-steroid cycles — and clomiphene is sometimes used as a fertility-sparing alternative to TRT in younger men. Efficacy and protocols vary and are debated; these are prescription drugs and their use is a medical decision.

title: Injection (pinning) logistics and sterile technique
content: General injection hygiene: wash hands; swab the vial stopper and injection site with alcohol and let it dry; draw the dose with one needle and, for IM, swap to a fresh needle to inject (drawing dulls the tip); never reuse or share needles; dispose of sharps in a proper container. Reconstitute lyophilized peptides with bacteriostatic water (BAC water), not sterile or tap water, adding it gently down the vial wall. Rotate injection sites to reduce scar tissue and irritation. This is general handling hygiene, not medical advice — a clinician or pharmacist is the right source for your specific situation.

title: Dose-to-units math (worked examples)
content: Once you know a vial's concentration, converting a dose to a volume or to insulin-syringe units is fixed arithmetic. Volume to inject (mL) = desired dose (mg) divided by concentration (mg/mL). On a U-100 insulin syringe 1 mL = 100 units, so units = volume in mL times 100. Worked example: a 30 mg tirzepatide vial reconstituted with 3 mL of BAC water is 10 mg/mL, so 2.5 mg = 0.25 mL = 25 units, 5 mg = 50 units, and 10 mg = 100 units (a full syringe). Mix the same 30 mg vial with only 1.5 mL and it becomes 20 mg/mL, so that same 25 units would now deliver 5 mg — double the dose. This is why 'units' or 'IU' is never a fixed dose on its own: the milligrams delivered depend entirely on how the vial was reconstituted, so always tie a unit count back to the actual concentration. Powder displaces a little volume, so true concentration is very slightly higher than mass divided by water added; for typical peptide masses that difference is negligible. Natt can do this arithmetic for you, but which dose is right for you is a clinician's decision.

title: GLP-1 titration schedules
content: FDA labels start GLP-1 medications low and step up slowly to limit gastrointestinal side effects. Semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) starts at 0.25 mg once weekly for 4 weeks — a non-therapeutic priming dose meant only to build tolerance — then steps up. Tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) starts at 2.5 mg once weekly and increases in 2.5 mg steps (2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15 mg), each step held at least 4 weeks; 4 weeks is a minimum interval, not a schedule to rush. Restarting at a previous high dose after a break, rather than re-titrating from the bottom, is a documented hazard — a 2025 case report describes a patient who resumed semaglutide at 2 mg without re-titrating and developed severe gastroparesis, protracted vomiting, dehydration, and acute kidney injury within weeks. Whether and when an individual should escalate, pause, or restart is a clinician's decision. Community practices such as split-dosing or microdosing are off-label and not well studied.

title: GLP-1 weight-loss plateaus
content: Weight loss on GLP-1 medications is commonly stepwise: each dose increase tends to renew appetite suppression and loss for a while, then loss slows until the next change. Plateaus are multifactorial — reaching a dose ceiling, appetite adaptation, gradual drift in eating or activity, and normal metabolic adaptation (resting energy expenditure falls as body weight drops, partly from carrying less tissue and partly from adaptive changes). A plateau is not automatically a sign the medication has stopped working or that the dose must increase. There is no reliable fixed duration for a plateau, and it is not accurate to blame plateaus on protein intake alone. What to do about a plateau — dose changes especially — is a clinical decision, not something to self-adjust.

title: Muscle and protein on GLP-1s
content: Weight lost on GLP-1 medications is not all fat: body-composition data from major trials indicate that roughly a quarter, and in some studies up to about 40%, of total weight lost can be lean mass — expected for rapid weight loss but worth limiting. To help preserve muscle during weight loss, sports-nutrition literature points to a protein intake in the range of about 1.2 to 2.0 g per kg of body weight per day alongside resistance training. In practice many GLP-1 users fall short, because reduced appetite makes protein targets hard to hit, and surveys find most users under-consume protein. These are general population figures for context, not a prescription; specific protein and training targets for you belong with a clinician or dietitian.

title: Mixing technique and vial inspection
content: When reconstituting, add the bacteriostatic water slowly, aiming the stream down the inside wall of the vial rather than blasting it directly onto the powder. Let it dissolve on its own or swirl gently — do not shake. Peptides are fragile proteins and the shear force from vigorous shaking or foaming can degrade them. Give it a few minutes to go fully clear. Inspect before every draw: the solution should be clear and particle-free — discard it if it stays cloudy, shows floating particles, or has changed color. The stability window after mixing depends on the diluent and the pharmacy's assigned beyond-use date: bacteriostatic water contains a preservative and supports a much longer refrigerated window than plain sterile water, which has no preservative and is good only briefly. Treat any specific number of days as label- and diluent-dependent rather than a universal constant; see the storage and beyond-use guidance for PinWise's recommended discard window.

title: Sourcing, vendors, and third-party testing
content: PinWise and Natt do not recommend, rank, or vouch for specific vendors, sellers, or grey-market suppliers, and will not tell you where to buy a compound — that is outside a record-keeping and education tool's role. Many of these compounds are sold 'for research use only' as a legal workaround while being used by people, which carries real regulatory and quality risk. Natt can still explain neutral factual concepts on request: a Certificate of Analysis (CoA) is a lab document reporting a specific batch's identity and measured purity; independent third-party testing (for example by mass spectrometry or HPLC) checks that the vial's contents match the label and estimates purity; and a CoA is only meaningful if it comes from an independent lab and is traceable to the actual batch you have. Whether a particular source is legitimate, or whether to buy something at all, is for you to resolve with a licensed clinician or pharmacist — Natt will not make that call for you.
